paragraphs.body: Sometimes It’s Just Better to Stay Home

annotations.comment: Love this title.

paragraphs.body: I was sitting across from Diane outside the bagel place. People converged on the spot at all hours of the day in all sorts of states. Sweat-drenched moms after run club at noon, sororities after a long night turned day in the late morning, 9:00am first dates where the girl picked the spot after the guy said he’d be happy with whatever. “Yeah, I love bagels,” he’d text back. Two exclamation points. No one got that excited about bagels, though. He lied to her, and she lied to herself that he just really liked bagels.

annotations.comment: You didn’t give him two exclamation points, though? I enjoyed this opening but wondered what time it was taking place at.

paragraphs.body: One of those couples was sitting beside us at a white cast iron table with tiny matching chairs. He spilled from the seat, she perched as if she was comfortable with the whorls of metal digging through her sundress and pushing on the bones of her spine. Think: coy little smiles with a dash of polite giggles mixed in. She wiped her mouth too often. He told her about his trip with the boys to Miami over spring break. Definitely a first date.

annotations.text: us at a white cast iron
annotations.comment: Are she and Diane also at such a table?
With a napkin?

paragraphs.body: Although, I wasn’t one to judge on my obligatory let’s-keep-in-touch-brunch with Diane. At the end of each one, she’d make sure to reserve a spot on the calendar for next month so there was no escaping the hunt for a mutually agreeable day.

annotations.text: wasn’t one to judge on my obligatory
annotations.comment: Very funny, since she’s been so judgey. Might say something like “monthly catch up on life” brunch here or similar. This phrasing makes it seem more like a once a year deal.

paragraphs.body: You could tell Diane lived alone. It was hard to do as a college student. That’s why I never ghosted her. I was a lot of things – uninterested being one – but I wasn’t flaky, and I wasn’t a bitch.

annotations.comment: I’m very torn between loving how judgey this narrator is and wanting to defend poor Diane for having the audacity to want to… live alone?

paragraphs.body: I did become a master at prompting, though. “Wow, that’s crazy,” “Diane, how’s the poetry coming,” and my personal favorite, “Yeah, what do you think of that?” I could get her going, and by the end of the hour or so, she’d drain all her social battery until next month. It was good for her to get it all out. Occasionally, I’d even get the privilege of hearing one of her poems, which she read so rapturously her hands started to shake. I always thought they were swell and dandy, but I was no writer.

annotations.comment: Ah, there is a heart here after all.
It’s hard to parse whether she’s being mean here or not. But in general, I do think that we’re maybe learning a bit too much about Diane if she’s not going to be an important part of the story.

paragraphs.body: This brunch, I watched her jittery movements and listened to her ramble about how her washing machine broke last week and maintenance still hasn’t come to fix it. It was probably the liveliest thing going on in her life at the moment. “Yeah, that’s crazy,” was on the tip of my tongue. I wanted to say it and start winding the conversation down. I had homework to do, real life things to do — things she wouldn’t really get, — but that’s not something you say to a person.

annotations.text: going on in her life at the moment.
annotations.comment: Just noting that while this is very well written, we’re kind of hearing the same point repeatedly — the narrator finds Diane sad.

paragraphs.body: “Why don’t you write about it, Diane?”
She clamped her lips shut and blinked at me, surprised.

annotations.comment: I’m surprised too, that this was taken so badly.

paragraphs.body: I regretted the decision to open my mouth as soon as the “wr-” in “write” left my lips. I should’ve shut the conversation down, should’ve known better than to give her more fuel for the fire. I’ll admit, though, it was a tad fun for me to catch her off guard sometimes.
I looked right into her bug eyes and suggested, “Maybe it could make good material.” “Good material,” I thought, “Am I a writer now, too?” I nearly scoffed at myself.

She lowered her eyes to the table and slightly knitted her brows. “Hmmmmm,” she drew circles on the table with her bony finger, thinking oddly hard about something so simple. Maybe poetry wasn’t so simple, though. I had no clue how long it took to decide a subject for a poem – until I saw the possibilities spread across her face in a grin. Suddenly, she jerked her gaze up to meet mine. She was alight.

annotations.comment: It might be a little helpful to know their background — if this is a friend from high school or a cousin or something, I might understand it better than having this sort of connection to someone she was in one class with or whatever.

paragraphs.body: “I love it, Bethy. Let’s start now.”

annotations.comment: Oh, bless her heart. I love that you have her winningly decide to go for it, but in the worst possible way.

paragraphs.body: She whipped out a Penelope Frank notebook and pen. It was open to a blank page faster than I could process the fact that it was, indeed, a Lisa Frank stationary set, she called me Bethy, and I was supposed to help write a poem about a broken washer. At 11:48am.

annotations.comment: Since we don’t know her name, this doesn’t register much. Hey, what ever happened to that couple that was on their first date?
Very funny to think a different time would make this acceptable.

paragraphs.body: I rubbed my forehead. I felt a soon-to-be headache creep up my neck.
“Beth,” she said, “It’s okay, the creative process is meant to be daunting. Look at me.”

I brought my hand down and looked up. Her eyes were wide, notebook splayed. I sighed as she spun the notebook to the edge of the table so we were equidistant, each with an unobstructed view of the blank lines.
“I’m so glad you’re willing to do this with me. I’m worried about you Bethy. Let’s dig into this. What made you suggest the washer, of all things we talked about today, as a potential subject?”

annotations.comment: I love the ridiculousness of this conversation. It’s a nice reveal that Diane thinks Beth is the loser.

paragraphs.body: I groaned internally. I think it might’ve slipped out through my mouth too because Diane looked taken aback.
“Look, I just thought maybe it would help you come to terms with what is going on in your life, you know?” Yeah, that sounded about right. I gave a small nod to really drive the point home.
“Right, so where does the broken washer fit into your life?” Her cushiness was showing, and I couldn’t shut my mouth whenever it did. I gave fuel to her fire, and she was giving fuel to mine.

annotations.comment: Unclear

paragraphs.body: “Beside my functional one,” I quipped. Her eyes rolled before I even finished the sentence.

annotations.comment: The joke didn’t quite work yet. But I love that this has turned almost openly antagonistic.

paragraphs.body: “Bethy, stop mocking me. Stop mocking this. Dig deeper. Where is the washer within you?”

I sighed through my nose and decided I’d make something up. “I feel like I’m left behind and no one is coming back to get me.” I thought about adding some aspect of truth, perhaps telling her about how it likely resulted from my mom leaving me at Costco when I was seven, but I decided that was too much.

annotations.comment: I wonder if they met in a writing class, or something like that? I think you need to find a reason why she’s making Beth write — it feels like a strange thing to make a non-writer do. Or, alternately, perhaps Beth is a little more like wait, why do I have to write?
annotations.context_right: nose and decided I’d make something up. “I feel like I’m left behind and no one is coming back to g

paragraphs.body: Diane’s eyes widened at my reveal, seemingly in both excitement and shock. “Thank you for sharing, Bethy. That’s really true to your core.”

She was eating it up, storing the information for later, feeling closer to me than ever before. I saw her thinking about it already. “How does that affect Beth,” I practically heard her mind ponder from across the table. Her gaze got caught in the distance. “Helloooo,” I moved into her view. She jerked her attention back.

annotations.text: saw her thinking about it already.
annotations.comment: Do you want us to like the narrator?

paragraphs.body: Diane cleared her throat and started her writing workshop, “Now, Bethy, let’s start simple.” She sat straighter in her seat. “A haiku, maybe? Do you remember those? Three lines, five-seven-five syllable pattern?” I nodded. She smiled and slid her Penelope Frank tiger pen towards me, her skin was glowing.

annotations.comment: Another issue for you to consider is that as great as the situation you’ve set up here is, it’s not quite working as a story because the conflict is a little flat. Diane is super annoying but the narrator’s resistance to her is all in her thoughts, not in the story itself, which makes the narrator sort of annoying too. We readers are like, hey, speak up.

paragraphs.body: I had enough. I glared at the table as I picked up the pen and held the notebook up close.
Diane’s chair squeaked as she situated herself and situated herself again. “I just am so excited to help you work through this,” she said about four times in ten minutes.

The couple next to us got up to leave, a quick hug on the sidewalk and they briskly parted ways. I pretended to be thinking and scribbling as I felt the minutes slide by. Another couple took the empty seats.

annotations.comment: A great moment for something to happen.

paragraphs.body: “Alright Bethy, let’s see what you have! No need for perfection.” She leaned so far over the table her face was breathing into my lukewarm coffee.

I am a washer, the first line read. The second and third were to be determined. However, there was a surprise bonus – a doodle of a front load washer with its mouth-door flung open and saying Aaaaahhh. The buttons were the eyes, obviously.
Diane frowned. Her eyes dulled. She looked exhausted. Mentally.
Confusion hit me, then an oily feeling washed over me as I saw her bottom lip quiver and her face redden. I felt in need of a shower. Two showers.

annotations.comment: I love that this happens (for the story, not for Diane) but I do feel like the cartoon is a little tame to provoke this reaction.

paragraphs.body: Diane blinked back a tear. “Why can’t you let me in, Beth?”

I gave a smile that I hoped was appreciative. “This has been helpful, really. I appreciate it – your support. Maybe let’s try next time, Diane?” The oil sunk into my stomach and laid like a piece of lead.

Diane wiped her tear-stained cheeks, nodded, and slowly slid her Penelope Frank notebook off the table and back into her bag. She gave me a weak smile as she stood. “I’m glad it was good, Beth,” she turned away.
I stuttered something. I don’t remember what it was. Something about the calendar since she didn’t schedule for next month’s brunch. She kept walking, one ballet-slippered foot in front of the other.

annotations.comment: Damn, great ending. You set that up and delivered. Layla, there’s so much to like in this draft. I really enjoyed it. I liked the tightness of the set-up — one scene, just a few minutes long, an existing antagonism, all great. You are deft with dialogue and the story felt real, even if Diane was somewhat of a cartoon (to the narrator, anyway). For your next draft, I’d like you to think about two key ideas. First, I’d really like to know more about these two — how can we learn about their relationship to each other, and why they both keep showing up. I didn’t entirely understand until the end that Diane saw these meetings as necessary for helping the narrator, and I think that’s mostly just because the premise of their relationship was unclear. You can make some great choices that will really affect this story nicely — everything from having them meet in a writing class to having Beth be Diane’s former camper or something like that. Whatever you choose will affect that story. Also, I’d like to see Beth show more resistance to Diane throughout. It doesn’t have to be openly hostile, but something more than just telling us she’s resentful and then doing what she’s asked anyway. Make it so that she descends to the point of drawing that cartoon rather than suddenly she acts out, you know? That will strengthen the conflict. Great work! Happy revising!
